q: The Russo-Swedish War of 1741–1743 ended with Russia occupying which territory?
e: The Russo-Swedish War of 1741–1743 (also called the Hats' War) ended with the Treaty of Åbo in 1743, by which Sweden ceded southeastern Finland to Russia. The conflict stemmed from Sweden's attempt to reclaim territory lost in the Great Northern War.

q: The War of the Fifth Coalition (1809) was fought primarily between which two powers?
e: The War of the Fifth Coalition (1809) was the last major conflict fought mainly between France under Napoleon and Austria. It ended with Napoleon's decisive victory at Wagram and the humiliating Treaty of Schönbrunn, forcing Austria to cede significant territory.

q: The First Schleswig War (1848–1851) was a conflict over which disputed region?
e: The First Schleswig War (1848–1851) was fought between the German Confederation and Denmark over control of the Duchy of Schleswig. The Danes were victorious, and Schleswig remained under the Danish crown, though tensions continued until the Second Schleswig War of 1864.

q: The First Serbian–Ottoman War (1876–1878) was triggered largely by uprisings in which region?
e: The First Serbian–Ottoman War (1876–1878) was sparked partly by the Herzegovina and Bosnia uprising of 1875 against Ottoman rule. Serbia, seeking to expand and aid the insurgents, declared war but suffered early defeats before Russia intervened, ultimately leading to Serbian and Montenegrin independence recognized at the Congress of Berlin.

q: The Battle of Noreia (113 BCE) was a defeat for which expanding Mediterranean republic?
e: The Battle of Noreia (113 BCE) was a catastrophic Roman defeat during the Cimbrian War, when the Roman consul Gnaeus Papirius Carbo was routed by the Germanic Cimbri tribe in the region of Noreia (modern Austria). It was one of the first major warnings of the Germanic threat to Rome.

q: The Battle of Iconium (1190) was fought during which crusade, as part of Frederick Barbarossa's overland march?
e: The Battle of Iconium (1190) was fought during the Third Crusade when Holy Roman Emperor Frederick Barbarossa's army defeated the Seljuk Turks and captured the city of Iconium (modern Konya). Barbarossa's subsequent drowning in the Saleph River decimated his crusading army before it reached the Holy Land.

q: The Battle of Achelous (917 CE) was a decisive victory for which empire over its Bulgarian rivals?
e: The Battle of Achelous (917 CE) was actually a catastrophic Byzantine defeat — Bulgarian Tsar Simeon I crushed the Byzantine forces near modern Pomorie, Bulgaria. It was one of the most decisive Bulgarian victories of the era, opening the road to Thrace and establishing Bulgarian supremacy in the Balkans.

q: The Battle of Wandiwash (1760) resulted in the decisive defeat of which European colonial power in India?
e: The Battle of Wandiwash (1760) was fought during the Seven Years' War in India, where British forces under Sir Eyre Coote decisively defeated the French under the Comte de Lally. The victory effectively ended French ambitions in India and paved the way for British dominance of the subcontinent.

q: The Battle of Galicia (1914) was a major early WWI offensive between Austria-Hungary and which opposing power?
e: The Battle of Galicia (August–September 1914) was one of the largest battles of the early First World War, in which Russian forces under Generals Ivanov and Ruzsky defeated Austro-Hungarian armies and captured much of eastern Galicia including Lwów. The campaign cost Austria-Hungary over 400,000 casualties.

q: The Battle of Papudo (1865) was a naval victory for which South American nation during the Chincha Islands War?
e: The Battle of Papudo (26 November 1865) was a naval engagement in which the Chilean corvette Esmeralda captured the Spanish gunboat Covadonga. It was one of the first Chilean naval victories of the war and became a source of national pride, with the Covadonga later serving as a Chilean warship.

q: The First Battle of the Masurian Lakes (1914) was fought on the Eastern Front between Germany and which enemy?
e: The First Battle of the Masurian Lakes (September 1914) was a German offensive that drove Russian forces from East Prussia following the German victory at Tannenberg. German commander Paul von Hindenburg inflicted heavy casualties on the Russian First Army, forcing a Russian retreat from German territory.

q: The Battle of Mühlberg (1547) was a victory for Holy Roman Emperor Charles V over which Protestant alliance?
e: The Battle of Mühlberg (24 April 1547) was a decisive Imperial victory during the Schmalkaldic War, in which Charles V's forces captured the Schmalkaldic League's leader, Elector John Frederick I of Saxony. The victory temporarily halted the spread of Lutheranism within the Empire and allowed Charles to impose the Augsburg Interim.

q: The Battle of St. Quentin (1871) took place during which Franco-German conflict?
e: The Battle of St. Quentin (19 January 1871) was one of the final engagements of the Franco-Prussian War, in which Prussian forces decisively defeated a French Army of the North. The defeat shattered the last major French field army and accelerated the armistice that ended the war.

q: The Battle of Amiens (1870) was an engagement during which 19th-century European war?
e: The Battle of Amiens (27 November 1870) was fought during the Franco-Prussian War when Prussian forces defeated the French Army of the North under General Faидherbe at Amiens. The Prussian victory helped consolidate their hold on northern France during the siege of Paris.

q: The Battle of Höchst (1622) was a Protestant defeat during which major European conflict?
e: The Battle of Höchst (20 June 1622) was fought during the Thirty Years' War, in which Imperial-Catholic League forces under Tilly defeated a Protestant army led by Christian of Brunswick attempting to link up with Frederick V's forces. The defeat devastated the Protestant cause in the Palatinate campaign.

q: The Battle of Sempach (1386) was a pivotal Swiss Confederation victory over which ruling dynasty?
e: The Battle of Sempach (9 July 1386) was a decisive Swiss victory over the Habsburgs near Sempach, Switzerland. According to legend, Arnold von Winkelried sacrificed himself by gathering a bundle of Habsburg spears to his chest, allowing the Swiss to break through the line. The battle secured Swiss independence from Habsburg overlordship.

q: The Battle of Wimpfen (1622) was a Protestant defeat during which major European conflict?
e: The Battle of Wimpfen (6 May 1622) was fought during the Thirty Years' War near Bad Wimpfen, where Catholic League forces under Tilly and Gonzalo de Córdoba defeated a Protestant army under the Margrave of Baden-Durlach. It was one of a series of Imperial victories that devastated the Protestant cause in southwestern Germany.

q: The Battle of Novara (1513) resulted in the expulsion of which power from the Duchy of Milan?
e: The Battle of Novara (6 June 1513) was a stunning victory by Swiss mercenaries fighting for the Milanese Sforza against French forces. The Swiss infantry's charge routed the French, who fled in disorder, and the Sforza were restored to Milan. It demonstrated the continued dominance of Swiss pike infantry in early 16th-century warfare.

q: The Battle of the Piave River (1918) was a failed Austrian offensive against which Allied nation?
e: The Battle of the Piave River (June 1918) was the last major Austro-Hungarian offensive of World War I against Italy. The attack across the Piave River was repulsed with heavy Austrian losses, and the failed offensive accelerated the collapse of the Austro-Hungarian army, which suffered a decisive defeat at Vittorio Veneto just months later.

q: The Battle of Vittorio Veneto (1918) effectively ended the war between Italy and which Central Power?
e: The Battle of Vittorio Veneto (24 October–4 November 1918) was the decisive Italian offensive that shattered the Austro-Hungarian army. The victory led directly to the Armistice of Villa Giusti on 3 November 1918, ending hostilities on the Italian Front and precipitating the dissolution of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.

q: The Battle of Holowczyn (1708) was a Swedish victory during which major Northern European conflict?
e: The Battle of Holowczyn (4 July 1708) was one of Charles XII of Sweden's finest tactical victories, in which Swedish forces crossed a flooded river under fire and routed a larger Russian army near modern Belarus. However, the subsequent campaign into Russia culminated in the decisive Swedish defeat at Poltava the following year.

q: The Battle of Zorndorf (1758) was a brutal engagement between Prussia and which opposing empire?
e: The Battle of Zorndorf (25 August 1758) was fought during the Seven Years' War between Frederick the Great's Prussian army and a Russian force under Fermor near modern Sarbinowo, Poland. The battle was extraordinarily bloody with both sides suffering massive casualties and neither achieving a clear victory, but it halted the Russian advance into Brandenburg.

q: The Battle of Mollwitz (1741) was the first major victory of which Prussian king, despite his own early flight from the field?
e: The Battle of Mollwitz (10 April 1741) was the opening battle of the First Silesian War, in which Prussian forces under Field Marshal Schwerin defeated Austria near modern Małujowice, Poland. Frederick II (the Great) initially fled the battlefield believing defeat was imminent, only to learn of his army's victory — a lesson that taught him never to leave a battle prematurely.

q: The Battle of Krefeld (1758) was a French defeat at the hands of which allied force during the Seven Years' War?
e: The Battle of Krefeld (23 June 1758) was fought during the Seven Years' War near Krefeld in the Rhineland, where an Anglo-Hanoverian army under Duke Ferdinand of Brunswick defeated a larger French force. The victory temporarily relieved pressure on Prussia's western flank and boosted Allied morale in the Western theatre.

q: Operation Swath-10 (1991) was a Croatian Army offensive against Territorial Defense forces during which armed conflict?
e: Operation Swath-10 (September 1991) was an early offensive by the Croatian Army (HV) against SAO Western Slavonia Territorial Defense Forces on Bilogora Mountain during the Croatian War of Independence. It was one of the Croatian military's first successful combined-arms operations in the newly independent republic's struggle against Serbian-backed forces.

q: The Battle of Pfaffenhofen (1745) was a Franco-Bavarian defeat during which 18th-century European war?
e: The Battle of Pfaffenhofen (15 April 1745) was fought during the War of the Austrian Succession near Pfaffenhofen an der Ilm in Bavaria. Austrian forces under Prince Liechtenstein defeated a Franco-Bavarian army, forcing the Elector of Bavaria to make peace and ending direct French intervention in southern Germany.

q: The Battle of Moys (1757) was a Prussian defeat during which major European conflict?
e: The Battle of Moys (7 September 1757) was fought during the Seven Years' War near Görlitz (modern Germany/Poland border), where Austrian forces under Marshal Nádasdy ambushed and defeated a Prussian corps under General Winterfeldt. Winterfeldt himself was mortally wounded — a significant personal loss for Frederick the Great, who regarded him as one of his ablest commanders.

q: The Battle of Ticonderoga (1759) resulted in the British capture of Fort Ticonderoga from which defending power?
e: The Battle of Ticonderoga (26–27 July 1759) saw British forces under General Jeffrey Amherst easily capture Fort Ticonderoga, which the French garrison had evacuated and partially blown up. The previous year's French victory at the same location (1758) had been one of Britain's worst defeats of the Seven Years' War in North America.

q: The Battle of Octodurus (56 BCE) was a Roman victory over the Veragri tribe in the territory of modern-day which country?
e: The Battle of Octodurus (56 BCE) was part of Caesar's Gallic War, fought at Martigny (ancient Octodurus) in the Valais region of modern Switzerland. Roman legions under Servius Galba defeated a Veragri and Seduni attack, securing Roman control of the Great St Bernard Pass route into Italy.

q: The Siege of Breslau (1945) was a prolonged German defensive action against forces of which nation?
e: The Siege of Breslau (January–May 1945) was one of the longest German defensive actions of the Second World War's Eastern Front. The city was declared a 'Festung' (fortress) by Hitler and held out against Soviet forces until 6 May 1945 — two days after Berlin's surrender — at enormous cost to both sides and the civilian population.

q: The Capture of Fidenae was an ancient Roman conquest of a rival city located how far from Rome?
e: Fidenae was an ancient Etruscan/Latin city located just 8 km north of Rome on the Via Salaria. It was repeatedly captured and revolted against Rome during the early Republic period (5th–4th century BCE), making it one of Rome's most frequently contested border settlements before the rise of Roman dominance in Latium.

q: The Second Battle of Agordat (1893) was an Italian colonial victory over the forces of which African state?
e: The Second Battle of Agordat (21 December 1893) was fought in Eritrea, where Italian colonial forces under General Arimondi defeated a Mahdist (Sudanese) force that had invaded Italian colonial territory. The Italian victory secured the frontier and was followed by an Italian advance to Kassala the following year.

q: The Battle of San Germano (1815) was a Neapolitan defeat during whose attempt to unify Italy against Austria?
e: The Battle of San Germano (2 May 1815) was fought during the Neapolitan War, when Joachim Murat — Napoleon's brother-in-law and King of Naples — marched north to expel Austrians from Italy and unite the peninsula. Austrian forces defeated his army at San Germano (modern Cassino), leading to Murat's capture and eventual execution.

q: The Battle of Näfels (1388) was a Swiss victory that, along with Sempach two years prior, secured independence from which ruling house?
e: The Battle of Näfels (9 April 1388) saw a small Swiss force from Glarus defeat a much larger Habsburg army that had invaded the canton, killing thousands of attackers. Together with the Battle of Sempach (1386), it confirmed the Swiss Confederation's military prowess and effectively ended Habsburg attempts to reassert control over the forest cantons.

q: The Battle of Gnila Lipa (1914) was an early WWI defeat for Austria-Hungary at the hands of which power?
e: The Battle of Gnila Lipa (26–30 August 1914) was fought during the opening weeks of WWI in Galicia, where Russian forces under General Ruzsky defeated Austro-Hungarian armies along the Gnila Lipa river. The defeat was part of a catastrophic series of Austro-Hungarian losses in the Battle of Galicia that cost the empire over 400,000 men in a single campaign.

q: The Battle of Laupen (1339) was a victory for Bern over a coalition that included which rival Swiss city-state?
e: The Battle of Laupen (21 June 1339) pitted the city of Bern and its forest allies against a coalition of the Burgundian nobility and Freiburg near the town of Laupen. Bern's victory established its military reputation and strengthened the early Swiss Confederation's resilience against noble aggression.

q: The Battle of Grandson (1476) during the Burgundian Wars was a defeat for which powerful duke?
e: The Battle of Grandson (2 March 1476) was one of the most stunning military defeats of the 15th century — the powerful Burgundian army of Charles the Bold was routed by Swiss Confederation forces and lost enormous amounts of treasure and artillery. It was the first of three catastrophic Burgundian defeats in 1476–1477 that would ultimately end Charles's ambitions and lead to his death at Nancy.

q: The Battle of Tempsford (917 CE) was a victory for the English kingdom of which royal house against Viking forces?
e: The Battle of Tempsford (917 CE) was a decisive victory for the forces of Edward the Elder of Wessex over the Danish Vikings, in which the Danish king Guthrum II and the Jarls of East Anglia were killed. The victory was a crucial step in Edward's campaign to reclaim the Danelaw and unify England under West Saxon rule.

q: The Siege of Compiègne (1430) resulted in the capture of which famous French military leader?
e: The Siege of Compiègne (May–October 1430) was Joan of Arc's last military action. She was captured by Burgundian forces during a skirmish outside the gates of Compiègne on 23 May 1430 when the drawbridge was raised before she could retreat. She was subsequently sold to the English and executed at Rouen in 1431.

q: The Battle of Harlem Heights (1776) saw American forces repel which European army in New York?
e: The Battle of Harlem Heights (16 September 1776) was one of George Washington's few early successes during the New York Campaign of the American Revolution. American forces — including elite rifle units — repulsed a British advance and inflicted significant casualties, boosting Continental morale after the disastrous defeat at the Battle of Long Island.

q: The Battle of Modlin (1939) was part of Nazi Germany's invasion of which country?
e: The Battle of Modlin (September 1939) was a siege in which German forces surrounded and bombarded the fortified Modlin Fortress during the invasion of Poland. The garrison of about 24,000 Polish troops under General Thommée held out until 29 September, surrendering only after running out of food and ammunition.

q: The Battle of Sultan Yacoub (1982) occurred during Israel's invasion of which neighboring country?
e: The Battle of Sultan Yacoub (11 June 1982) was fought during Israel's First Lebanon War near the Syrian border in the Bekaa Valley. Israeli armored forces suffered significant losses in an engagement against Syrian troops, and three Israeli soldiers were captured — their fate remained unknown for decades and became a major Israeli national issue.

q: The Battle of Skalitz (1866) was a Prussian victory during the brief war between Prussia and which empire?
e: The Battle of Skalitz (28 June 1866) was fought during the Austro-Prussian (Seven Weeks') War near Česká Skalice in Bohemia. Prussian forces under Crown Prince Frederick William defeated Austrian I Corps, inflicting heavy casualties and contributing to the rapid Prussian advance that culminated in the decisive Battle of Königgrätz just days later.

q: The Battle of Crevola (1487) was a defeat for Swiss mercenaries at the hands of forces from which Italian duchy?
e: The Battle of Crevola (28 April 1487) was fought in the Val d'Ossola near Crevoladossola (modern Piedmont), where Milanese forces ambushed and routed a marauding Swiss army that had crossed the Alps. It was one of the few significant Swiss military defeats of the era and temporarily checked Swiss expansion southward into the Po Valley.

q: The Gorlice–Tarnów Offensive (1915) was a major Central Powers breakthrough against which Allied nation?
e: The Gorlice–Tarnów Offensive (May–September 1915) was one of the most successful military operations of the First World War, in which combined German-Austrian forces under Mackensen broke through the Russian lines in Galicia. The offensive resulted in Russian forces being pushed out of Poland and Galicia, with Russia losing over one million men and suffering a catastrophic strategic defeat.

q: The Battle of Maclodio (1427) was a decisive defeat for which Italian city-state's military forces?
e: The Battle of Maclodio (11 October 1427) was fought during the wars between Venice and Milan, in which Venetian forces under Francesco Bussone da Carmagnola thoroughly defeated the Milanese army. The victory resulted in the capture of a large part of the Milanese cavalry and forced Milan to cede significant territory to Venice and Florence in the subsequent Treaty of Ferrara.

q: The Battle of Königsberg (1945) resulted in the fall of this East Prussian city to which army?
e: The Battle of Königsberg (6–9 April 1945) was a Soviet assault during the East Prussian Offensive that captured the heavily fortified German city of Königsberg (modern Kaliningrad, Russia). After three days of intense urban combat following a prolonged siege, German commander Otto Lasch surrendered despite Hitler's orders to hold to the last — for which Lasch was sentenced to death in absentia.

q: The Battle of Liège (1914) was the first major engagement of WWI on what front?
e: The Battle of Liège (5–16 August 1914) was the opening engagement of the German invasion of Belgium during WWI, as part of the Schlieffen Plan. The Belgian garrison under Gérard Leman held out far longer than expected, delaying the German advance and giving the Entente crucial time to mobilize. The stubborn defense became a symbol of Belgian resistance.

q: The Battle of Sinsheim (1674) was a French victory during which major European conflict?
e: The Battle of Sinsheim (16 June 1674) was fought during the Franco-Dutch War, in which French Marshal Turenne defeated an Imperial-German force near Sinsheim in the Rhineland. Turenne's subsequent ravaging of the Palatinate to deny the enemy a base of operations remains controversial and caused massive civilian suffering.

q: L'Escalade commemorates a failed 1602 night assault on Geneva by the forces of which neighboring state?
e: L'Escalade ('the scaling of the walls') of 11–12 December 1602 was a failed night attack by Savoyard forces attempting to capture Geneva by scaling its walls with ladders. The attack was repulsed by the city's defenders. The event is celebrated annually in Geneva with costumed parades, and tradition holds that a widow named Mère Royaume broke a pot of hot soup over the head of an invading soldier.
